_comment: One file per procedure_code. Distilled from the matching pair in PM JAY/Standard Treatment Guideline/master/<condition>/ : _Medco.pdf (clinical + mandatory docs) + _PPD.CPD.pdf (PPD/CPD processing checklists). Filename = procedure_code. This is what the Documentation Checker, Query Predictor, and Medical Justification agents read AFTER the picker has chosen the code.
min_doctor_qualification: Surgical expertise skills appropriate for performing laparoscopic procedures
clinical_indications: A cyst with significant pain and other features suggestive of rupture/torsion
Ovarian cystic structure >7cm without regression for 6-8wks
Palpable adnexal mass in postmenopausal patients

preauth.label: Detailed Clinical notes with history, indications, symptoms, signs, examination findings and advice for admission
Other Tumor markers based on etiology (Optional)

ppd_preauth.q: Detailed Clinical notes all vitals, detailed history, symptoms, signs, physical examination including local examination, indication for procedure, planned line of treatment, and advice for admission?
Did physical examination, imaging and investigation confirm the diagnosis?
Was there an evidence of cysts/tumor on Ultrasound report?

cpd_claim.q: Are the detailed ICPs with daily vitals and treatment details?
Are the detailed procedure / Operative Notes available?
Was the imaging indicative of surgery?
Was malignancy ruled out in suspicious lesions?
Is the discharge summary with follow-up advise at the time of discharge?

common_queries: Physical examination or imaging and investigation was not indicative of surgery.
Missing detailed clinical notes including history, indications, symptoms, signs, examination findings, planned line of treatment, or advice for admission.
Diagnosis not confirmed by physical examination, imaging, or investigation reports.
No evidence of cysts or tumor on the Ultrasound report.
Detailed ICPs with daily vitals and treatment details are missing or incomplete.
Detailed procedure or operative notes are not available.
Malignancy was not ruled out in suspicious lesions.
Discharge summary or follow-up advice at the time of discharge is missing or incomplete.
Ovarian mass >10cm not documented, or documented below threshold without justification.
Ovarian cystic structure >7cm without regression for 6-8wks not documented, or regression was noted.
CA 125 Tumor marker or other tumor markers (if indicated) missing or incomplete at pre-authorization.

clinical_key_pointers: Ovarian cysts can occur at any stage in life from foetal life through menopause. They can be symptomatic or asymptomatic and found incidentally on clinical exam or on imaging.
It is important to differentiate between benign and malignant enlargements of the ovary to institute timely and effective treatment without undue delay.
The optimal surgical goal is to remove the entire cyst intact. The cyst should be removed inside a laparoscopic bag so that inadvertent spillage into the peritoneal cavity may be avoided.

Ovarian conservation is generally the goal in a premenopausal woman with a benign ovarian cyst requiring surgical excision. The advantage is preservation of viable ovarian tissue and thus fertility and hormone production.
Ovarian cysts can be symptomatic or asymptomatic.
Symptoms that women may experience include unilateral pain or pressure in the lower abdomen.
Pain may be intermittent or constant and characterized as sharp or dull.
If an ovarian cyst ruptures or ovarian torsion is present, the patient may experience a sudden onset of acute severe pain, possibly associated with nausea and vomiting.
The menstrual cycle can become irregular, and abnormal vaginal bleed may occur.

For simple cysts in premenopausal women, 2/3rd regress over 2-3 menstrual cycles; a 'Wait and See' policy for 8-10 weeks is recommended. OCPs for 3 cycles have no proven benefit. Aspiration is not useful due to 75% recurrence within 1 year.

For simple cysts of 5-7 cm in low-risk patients & 2-7 cm in high-risk patients with certain benign nature, yearly follow-up is required until resolved. Cysts >7cm need further evaluation with MRI/Surgery.
If conservative management fails for benign conditions and surgical intervention is decided, efforts should be made to conserve normal ovarian tissue by ovarian cystectomy.
Three classic complications of ovarian cysts that commonly present to the emergency department are Rupture, Haemorrhage, and Torsion.
